item.title: Awww-inspiring historical skip
item.content:encoded: Laika staying lovable, trusting humans despite being left to die in space — now that's being man's best friend. Her acting realistically canine in the dream incidents was a clever touch, too…subverting the "dream animals can do anything" expectation. Great job overall!

item.title: Many doggo
item.content:encoded:
I love doggos. I love space doggos. I am sad that space doggo dead.

+1

item.title: Re: borf
item.content:encoded: Godspeed, good girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm,

It is believed that SCP-2624 was the result of a malfunction within a paratechnological communication system,

Maybe GRU 'P' had a hand in the creation of this anomaly in the first place..? (And the crosslink puts a little extra weight behind that suspicion.)

It is unclear whether the malfunction was a result of Soviet error or of sabotage by United States intelligence agencies.

Maybe GRU 'P' wanted to sever connection from their own mistake? Or maybe they felt that if the US caused this mess, an organization based over there should take on the cost and work of dealing with it?

Now, I'm not sayin… I'm just sayin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice skip! But since this is from Russia, wouldn't it be good to at least mention Gru Division P before the Foundation is asked to step in?

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edit
- Hide

To link to a similar article, I propose the folllowing edit:

It is believed that SCP-2624 was the result of a malfunction within a paratechnological communication system

to

It is believed that SCP-2624 was the result of a malfunction within a paratechnological communication system

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is heartwarming. Glad to see Laika is doing well, I always felt sorry for her due to her fate. ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…wait. The containment procedures…

The Foundation faked the moon landings, but not to fake actually landing on the moon? That is hilariously brilliant!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dogs and space are a few of my favorite things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does nothing for me. It's just a bunch of dogs. In space. And they show up in a bunch of weird places. There's no interesting story or effect or hook or punchline or anything. Just dogs. Dogs as far as the eye can see. Dogs all the way down.
At first I thought this is a J-Article but after reading it completely I am a bit terrified by a big dog-ball in space which uses dogs for propulsion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
A very creative idea. This entity is unnerving in nature, but at the same time, it's the only one I can think of that intentionally saved someone's life.

item.title: Godspeed Noble Doggo
item.content:encoded: That is adorable omg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for sheer wtf factor

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tries to imagine a dog explaining complex machinery. Upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The object class system is inherently subjective and varies based on headcanon. 2624 is very expensive for the Foundation to contain and the cost is only going to increase in the future, along with the risks of causing an information leak — increase so much that the Foundation is actually going to try to destroy this thing. In my mind that's a pretty clear qualifier for Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Difficulty of containment doesn't necessarily make something Keter.

Whether or not the object poses a clear and present threat to the world/reality at-large is what defines a Keter.

This is strange and currently uncontained, but not immediately dangerous; making it Euclid, at least given my understanding of how the classification system works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's very hard to contain.

Object classes mostly have to do with level of how easy it is to contain. This is a freakin giant orb of dogs in space, someone's gonna know! Also, keters can be benificial, just look at SCP-2702!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I missed something, but why is this Keter?

Every encounter with it has either been beneficial, or neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Relevant.

Also relevant, but more weeaboo.

+1 - I personally liked it. The depiction of Laikas attempting to convey advanced tech to astronauts was amusing, and the 2000 event managed to both be heartwarming, sad, and funny.

Also, correcting orbit by ejecting dogs. :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…so it's a mass of doggos floating around the earth, capable of ejecting more doggos and cause doggos to spawn out of nowhere.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does anyone else see this as a perfect opportunity for the Administrator to finally get a pet?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Laika, famous space doggo from Russia, is now many space doggos. Would cuddle all of them tenderly. Rated 940/600'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Łajka skip. I must upvote, comrade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Tawarisz, it's a dog in space!"
"And?"
"…a dog…. in space…"
"Is it dangerous or in any other way interesting for Motherland?"
"No, tawarisz."
"Then why should I care?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because DC Comics' Dogwelder and my current Tshirt (although is an astronaut cat, not a dog…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered this, but ended up deciding that a more restrained and concise approach would be more effective. Which is probably for the best, since my standard skip strategy is "add interview logs until everything's explained".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All things considered, I think the events mentioned in the addendum would have more impact if they were accounts from the people involved. I mean, summaries are nice, but it could be better to hear them from the horse's mouth (metaphorically speaking).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh shit. I just got the implication too. That's pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I had just gotten it out of my head. Dammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the Foundation arranges the dogs that continue to fall from outer space?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm scared of space, and love dogs… and now I am conflicted on whether or not I want to go to space to meet helpful space doggo. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Understood, but… there's a difference between the effects of an SCP being widespread and being pervasive to the point of self-aggrandizement. Laika showing up and even interacting with astronauts on a regular basis as described makes for cool stories, and I can buy it. "Basically everyone whose ever been to space hallucinates or dreams about this" has none of the inherent coolness of those events, and manages to cheapen space flight in general. It's like how folks critique a bad compulsion effect: regardless of who you are and why you're going, that story begins and ends when you go to space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will point out that a lot of people who go to space get used to experiencing weird stuff with mundane explanations: seeing flashes of light from cosmic rays passing through the fluid in their eyes, legitimately creepy radio signals that come from weird spots in the magnetosphere, all the weird space debris that the internet declares UFO's… add that to the chronic sleep problems a lot of them experience, and you get a group of people with a really strong weirdness censor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote. (Plz don't kill me dog people.)

I really enjoyed the idea of the living ball of shy dogs around Laika and found most of the object's history quite interesting, but phantom dogs randomly appearing in one form or another to the majority of people who have been in space blew away my sense of immersion1 and didn't feel like it was thematically connected to the SCP by anything besides being "sad space dog stuff." (E.g., crazy dog satellite, space dog dreams, and Laika helping or interacting with astronauts all feel like distinct, unconnected stories.)

Footnotes
1. It's clear that I share a passion for space travel with many here, but this affecting at minimum 80% of people in space actually ruins this skip for me because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "There is self-replicating bear in red square. Space dog is fine in space."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Believe me when I say I'm working on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Multiplying dogs with seemingly supernatural abilities… is this whole thing a Dog Residue reference?

Regardless, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doggo canon, anybody?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We already have Bisexual Spirit Doggos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
60 living dogs

Just about killed me. And I love the explanation of -2 and the second -3 manifestation. Fantastic stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's next, bisexual accountant doggos? Asexual halloween doggos?!

Wait a second

subway doggos

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lots to love here. Damn shame I can only +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And now, Yuri!!! in Space

First we had lesbian hunger doggos and now we have gay space doggos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is cute and somewhat sad, and incredibly endearing. Upvoted.

crewed by Yuri Gagarin on April 12, 1961

*gigglesnort* And now, Yuri!!! in Space

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Thumbs up* Simple but works well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most downright hilarious non-J skips I've read in a long time. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted immediately at

Due to the dogs' lack of vocal cords or opposable thumbs, these dreams primarily consist of them destroying the machinery in question with their teeth while barking incessantly.

They're just trying to help!

item.title: Godspeed, noble doggo
item.content:encoded: A true hero of Earth and beyond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That'll do, Laika. That'll do. ;-;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
people who have traveled to space post-SCP-2624

I appreciate this implication a great deal.

The article is fantastic, too, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoughts
hide

Upvoted at this line:

SCP-2624 is capable of ejecting singular dogs at velocities exceeding 10 km/s as propulsion to alter its orbit and counteract orbital decay.

I love seeing Laika written about, she was actually the focus of my team's entry in the Groups Of Interest contests, and I love Soviet Union and space travel history. This just appeals to me on a lot of different levels. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of picture GRU-P's reaction being along the lines of "Eh, just space dog. Is fine."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, my only question is why Gru Division-P isn't involved or even mentioned at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They just want to space-fetch and space-help!

2624's method of propulsion is the best thing I've read on this site in a while. It's both incredibly sad and adorable and hilarious.

space doggos!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noo don't decommission space doggos they're doing their best +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Laika SCPs get my vote.

I'm interpreting this as a revenge of sorts by Laika for killing her, I mean Oleg Gazenko, one of the chief scientists responsible for Sputnik 2, did express regret over Laika many years later, when he said this;

"Work with animals is a source of suffering to all of us. We treat them like babies who cannot speak. The more time passes, the more I’m sorry about it. We shouldn’t have done it. We did not learn enough from the mission to justify the death of the dog."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AUGH POOR BABY
+1

item.title: Re: borf
item.content:encoded: Godspeed, noble doggo.

item.title: godspeed, noble doggo
item.content:encoded: This is the cutest thing I've read in a while.
